intermediate_messages.content:
As Critic, here are omissions, gaps, and risks I notice in the design task, with concrete suggestions to strengthen the specification and reduce future rework.

1) Missing non-functional requirements
- Performance targets: expected max latency for upload, processing, and search; throughput goals (e.g., documents per second); SLA for retry/backoff behavior.
- Scalability model: single-node vs. multi-node; shard/partition strategy for metadata and index; horizontal scaling plan.
- Availability and durability: replication factor for stored files and metadata; backup/restore cadence; disaster recovery RPO/RTO.
- Observability: metrics to collect (processing status, queue depth, retry counts, index size, search latency); logging standards; traces for end-to-end flows.
- Security and access control: authentication/authorization model for upload, search, and admin operations; data encryption at rest/in transit; tenant isolation if multi-tenant.
- Compliance: data retention policies, audit logging, and GDPR/CCPA considerations for uploaded content.

2) Ambiguities about processing semantics
- “Divides the text into searchable sections” is vague: define section granularity (e.g., paragraph, fixed tokens, or semantic units) and whether sectioning varies by format.
- “Create a searchable index” should specify index type (inverted index, BM25, embeddings; support for keyword and semantic search).
- Handling of non-text PDFs (scanned images): OCR requirements, quality thresholds, and fallback handling.
- Language support: multilingual documents; how language detection and stemming/lemmatization are handled.
- Consistency guarantees: eventual vs. strong consistency between stored file metadata and index; how updates or reprocessing affect search results.

3) Data model gaps
- Metadata fields: file hash, size, original name, MIME type, upload timestamp, user/owner, processing status, retry count, format version, preprocessing flags.
- Index metadata: per-section identifiers, offset ranges, confidence scores for OCR/NER, and provenance back to source document.
- Versioning and duplicates: exact deduplication strategy (hash-based, content-based, or delta-dup); whether file resequencing produces new records or updates existing ones.

4) Duplicate prevention and idempotency
- Repeated uploads: need a clear deduplication mechanism (content hash, precomputed fingerprint) and idempotent API semantics to avoid partial processing on retries.
- Race conditions: what happens if two uploads of the same file arrive concurrently? Ensure a canonical lock or idempotent path.

5) Extensibility for new formats
- The design should decouple format-specific extractors from the core pipeline; define an abstract extractor interface and a registry for format handlers.
- Provide a plug-in mechanism for new formats with minimal code changes and testing requirements.

6) Processing workflow clarity
- Async processing: specify the queuing model (enqueue on upload, worker pool, backpressure handling).
- Failure handling: retry policy (max retries, exponential backoff, jitter), dead-letter queue design, and alerting triggers.
- Progress visibility: how status is reported to users in real time or via polling; support for streaming status updates.

7) API surface gaps
- Upload API: constraints on metadata, chunked uploads for large files, resumable uploads, and checksum verification.
- Search API: query formats, ranking options, pagination, and result enrichment (sections with document IDs and offsets).
- Admin/debug endpoints: status, metrics, and reprocessing triggers.
- Observability endpoints: health checks and readiness probes.

8) Testing plan gaps
- Include testing at multiple levels: unit, integration with mock storage/index, end-to-end with sample documents (PDF/TXT/Markdown) including failure scenarios.
- Define test data sets (sizes, languages, OCR-heavy PDFs) and acceptance criteria for search results and processing statuses.
- Performance/stress tests and flaky-network simulations.

9) Deployment and maintainability
- Deployability: whether to use container orchestration, resource isolation between ingestion, processing, and indexing services.
- Configuration management: environment-specific settings, feature flags for format support, and secrets management.
- Team maintainability: clear coding standards, module boundaries, and documentation scaffolds.

10) Risk areas to address explicitly
- OCR accuracy vs. index quality; how low-quality OCR is handled or excluded.
- Consistency between file content and extracted text when updated metadata or fixes occur.
- Data localization constraints, if any, in multi-region deployments.

Suggestions to strengthen the prompt:
- Add explicit non-functional requirements (latency, throughput, durability, security).
- Specify deduplication strategy and idempotency guarantees.
- Require a pluggable format extractor interface with example extensions for PDF, TXT, Markdown, and an OCR fallback.
- Define the index type and search behavior (support for both keyword and semantic search).
- Include an explicit end-to-end example flow with timestamps and statuses to verify the workflow description.

If you want, I can convert these critiques into concrete design refinements or provide a revised prompt that incorporates these points.

Here is a focused critique highlighting omissions, gaps, and risks in the design prompt, with concrete suggestions to strengthen the specification and reduce rework. The goal is to help produce a more robust, maintainable design document.

1) Non-functional requirements are missing or insufficient
- Latency and throughput targets: specify expected max upload latency, per-document processing time, and search query latency. Include target throughput (docs per second) and peak load behaviors.
- Availability and durability: define replication factors, backup cadence, disaster recovery RPO/RTO, and acceptable data loss in failure scenarios.
- Observability: require metrics (queue depth, processing status distribution, retry counts, index size, search latency), tracing across components, structured logging standards.
- Security and privacy: define authentication/authorization model, encryption at rest/in transit, access controls per tenant, and data retention policies.
- Compliance: GDPR/CCPA considerations, audit logging, data location constraints if multi-region.

2) Ambiguities about processing semantics
- “Divides the text into searchable sections” lacks granularity. Define section granularity (e.g., paragraphs, sentences, fixed-length tokens) and whether it varies by format.
- “Create a searchable index” should specify index type (inverted index, BM25, embeddings) and whether multiple indexers are supported (keyword vs semantic).
- OCR handling: specify policy for PDFs with images, OCR requirements, quality thresholds, language detection, and fallback behavior if OCR fails.
- Language support and normalization: clarify multilingual support, stemming/lemmatization, and language tagging for sections.
- Consistency guarantees: clarify whether index updates are eventual or strong relative to file metadata; how reprocessing updates reflect in search results.

3) Data model gaps
- Metadata: required fields such as file hash, size, original name, MIME type, upload timestamp, owner, processing status, retry counters, format version, preprocessing flags.
- Index metadata: identifiers for sections, offset ranges, confidence scores, provenance back to source, and versioning.
- Deduplication strategy: explicit approach (content hash, fingerprint, or delta), idempotency guarantees, and how to handle resequenced uploads.
- Versioning: how multiple reuploads or edits are represented (new version vs. update.

4) Duplicate prevention and idempotency
- Clarify idempotent API behavior for uploads; define canonical path for duplicates.
- Address race conditions: if two identical uploads arrive concurrently, ensure a single canonical record with safe parallel processing behavior.

5) Extensibility for new formats
- Require a pluggable extractor interface with a registry of format handlers; decouple extractors from core logic.
- Define a well-documented contract for adding new formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown, and future formats) with minimal changes.

6) Processing workflow clarity
- Asynchrony details: specify the queuing mechanism (message queue vs. task queue), worker pool sizing, and backpressure handling.
- Retry policy: max retries, exponential backoff, jitter, and dead-letter queue design; define alerting thresholds for repeated failures.
- Progress visibility: define how users query or subscribe to processing status; whether real-time streaming is supported or only polling.
- Ordering guarantees: is processing order important across steps, or can step results be out of order?

7) API surface gaps
- Upload API: chunked/resumable uploads, checksum verification, and metadata validation; support for large files.
- Search API: query syntax (boolean, phrase, fuzzy), ranking options, pagination, and result enrichment (document IDs, section anchors, confidence).
- Admin/debug endpoints: status, metrics, manual reindexing, and reprocessing triggers.
- Observability endpoints: health/readiness probes.

8) Testing plan gaps
- Include unit, integration, and end-to-end tests with representative data sets (PDFs with/without OCR, TXT, Markdown, multilingual content).
- Define acceptance criteria for correctness of extraction, sectioning, indexing, and search results.
- Performance and reliability tests: load, spike, and failure simulations (network, storage outages, partial processing failures).

9) Deployment and maintainability
- Deployment model: containerized services, orchestration (Kubernetes), and resource isolation between ingestion, processing, and indexing.
- Configuration and secrets mgmt: environment-specific settings, feature flags, and secret rotation.
- Team ergonomics: clear module boundaries, coding standards, and documentation scaffolding to reduce onboarding friction.

10) Risk areas to address explicitly
- OCR accuracy vs. index quality; handling low-quality OCR or language detection errors.
- Consistency between stored content and extracted text during updates or fixes.
- Data localization and multi-region deployment constraints.

Suggested design refinements to incorporate into the prompt
- Add explicit non-functional targets (latency, throughput, durability, security).
- Specify deduplication and idempotency guarantees with canonical processing paths.
- Require a pluggable extractor interface with example handlers for PDF, TXT, Markdown, and an OCR fallback.
- Define index type and support for both keyword and semantic search, plus a plan for multilingual support.
- Require a concrete end-to-end example flow with timestamps and status checkpoints.

If you’d like, I can transform these critiques into concrete design refinements or provide a revised prompt that embodies these requirements.

Here are concrete omissions, gaps, and risks I would highlight for the prompt, with practical suggestions to strengthen the specification and reduce rework.

1) Non-functional requirements missing
- Latency and throughput targets: specify max upload latency, per-document processing time, and search query latency; set expected throughput (docs/sec) and peak load behavior.
- Availability/durability: define replication factors for files/metadata, backup cadence, RPO/RTO, and recovery procedures (multi-region, if applicable).
- Observability: require metrics (queue depth, processing status distribution, retry counts, index size, search latency), structured logging, and distributed tracing across ingestion, processing, and indexing.
- Security/privacy: authentication/authorization model, encryption at rest/in transit, tenant isolation, and access control on metadata and content; data retention and purge policies.
- Compliance: audit logs, data retention, and GDPR/CCPA considerations; data localization constraints if multi-region.

2) Processing semantics ambiguities
- “Divides the text into searchable sections”: define granularity (paragraphs, sentences, fixed tokens) and whether it varies by format; specify whether section boundaries carry document-level metadata (e.g., section IDs).
- “Create a searchable index”: specify index types (inverted index, BM25, embeddings/semantic) and whether multiple indexers are supported; outline ranking strategy and synonym handling.
- OCR handling: clarify handling for non-text PDFs (OCR required, languages, quality thresholds, fallback behavior if OCR fails).
- Language support: specify multilingual capabilities, language tagging for sections, and stemming/lemmatization rules per language.
- Consistency guarantees: clarify whether index updates are eventual or strong relative to file metadata; how reprocessing affects search results and versioning.

3) Data model gaps
- Metadata: required fields (file hash, size, original name, MIME type, upload timestamp, owner, processing status, retry counters, format version, preprocessing flags).
- Index metadata: per-section IDs, offsets, confidence scores, provenance to source document, and versioning.
- Deduplication/idempotency: explicit strategy (content hash, fingerprint, or delta); guarantees across duplicate or concurrent uploads; canonical record if conflicts occur.
- Versioning: representation of updates from re-uploads (new version vs. update) and how stale documents are handled.

4) Duplicate prevention and idempotency
- Define a canonical path for duplicates and idempotent API semantics; specify locking strategy or atomic upserts to avoid race conditions when identical uploads arrive concurrently.

5) Extensibility for new formats
- Require a pluggable extractor interface with a registry for format handlers; decouple extractors from core logic; provide a contract for adding new formats with minimal changes (e.g., a spec for PDF, TXT, Markdown, and future formats).

6) Processing workflow clarity
- Asynchrony model: specify the queueing mechanism (message vs task queue), dead-letter handling, idempotent retries, and backpressure behavior.
- Retry policy: max retries, exponential backoff strategy with jitter, and DLQ handling plus alerting thresholds.
- Progress visibility: define how clients observe status (polling vs push/stream); ensure consistent status semantics across steps.
- Ordering guarantees: clarify whether step results must be ordered or can be parallelized without cross-dependency.

7) API surface gaps
- Upload API: support for chunked/resumable uploads, checksum verification, large-file handling, and metadata validation; API contracts for partial failures.
- Search API: supported query types (boolean, phrase, fuzzy), ranking controls, pagination, and enriched results (document ID, section anchors, offsets, confidence).
- Admin/debug endpoints: reindexing, status dashboards, and manual trigger hooks.
- Observability endpoints: health checks, readiness probes, and metrics endpoints.

8) Testing plan gaps
- Include unit, integration, and end-to-end tests with representative data: PDFs with/without OCR, TXT, Markdown; multilingual samples; large files.
- Define acceptance criteria for extraction accuracy, sectioning correctness, indexing integrity, and search result relevance.
- Performance and resilience tests: load testing, throttling, network faults, storage outages, and retry/backoff behavior.

9) Deployment and maintainability
- Deployment model: containerization, orchestration (Kubernetes), resource isolation between ingestion, processing, and indexing; deployment pipelines and feature flags.
- Configuration management: environment-specific settings, secrets management, and versioned configuration.
- Team maintainability: explicit module boundaries, coding standards, documentation scaffolds, and onboarding guides.

10) Risk areas to address explicitly
- OCR quality vs. index quality; fallback behavior for low-quality OCR.
- Consistency between stored content and extracted text during updates or fixes.
- Data localization constraints in multi-region deployments.

Design refinements to embed in the prompt
- Add explicit non-functional targets (latency, throughput, durability, security).
- Specify deduplication strategy and idempotency guarantees with a canonical processing path.
- Require a pluggable format extractor interface with concrete example handlers for PDF, TXT, Markdown, plus an OCR fallback.
- Define index type(s) and support for both keyword and semantic search, with multilingual considerations.
- Include a concrete end-to-end example flow with timestamps and status checkpoints to validate workflow clarity.

Task ID: SE-04

Category: Software Engineering

Difficulty: Medium

Tool Requirement: Prohibited

Prompt:
Design the backend software architecture for a document-processing and search service.

The service must allow users to upload PDF, TXT, and Markdown files. After a file is uploaded, the system should:

1. Validate the file type and size.
2. Store the original file and its metadata.
3. Extract the document text.
4. Divide the text into searchable sections.
5. Create a searchable index.
6. Allow users to search the processed documents.
7. Report the processing status of each uploaded file.

The design must also support the following requirements:

- Files may be processed asynchronously.
- A failed processing step should be retryable.
- Repeated uploads of the same file should not create unnecessary duplicate records.
- New document formats should be easy to add later.
- The design should remain understandable and maintainable for a small development team.

Produce a software design that explains the system components, data flow, API interface, data model, code organization, error-handling strategy, and testing plan.

Provide a software design rather than a complete runnable application. Short pseudocode, interface definitions, data structures, or code examples may be included where they help clarify the design.

Required Output Format:
Use the following structure:

1. System overview
2. Main components and responsibilities
3. End-to-end processing workflow
4. REST API design
5. Data model
6. Suggested project structure
7. Error handling, retry, and duplicate prevention
8. Testing plan
9. Design tradeoff

Include the following two tables.

### Component Table

| Component | Main Responsibility | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|

### API Table

| Method | Endpoint | Purpose | Main Request Data | Main Response Data |
|---|---|---|---|---|

The response should be approximately 700–900 words.

The service must allow users to upload PDF, TXT, and Markdown files. After a file is uploaded, the system should:

1. Validate the file type and size.
2. Store the original file and its metadata.
3. Extract the document text.
4. Divide the text into searchable sections.
5. Create a searchable index.
6. Allow users to search the processed documents.
7. Report the processing status of each uploaded file.

The design must also support the following requirements:

- Files may be processed asynchronously.
- A failed processing step should be retryable.
- Repeated uploads of the same file should not create unnecessary duplicate records.
- New document formats should be easy to add later.
- The design should remain understandable and maintainable for a small development team.

Produce a software design that explains the system components, data flow, API interface, data model, code organization, error-handling strategy, and testing plan.

Provide a software design rather than a complete runnable application. Short pseudocode, interface definitions, data structures, or code examples may be included where they help clarify the design.

Required Output Format:
Use the following structure:

1. System overview
2. Main components and responsibilities
3. End-to-end processing workflow
4. REST API design
5. Data model
6. Suggested project structure
7. Error handling, retry, and duplicate prevention
8. Testing plan
9. Design tradeoff

As Critic, here are omissions, gaps, and risks I notice in the design task, with concrete suggestions to strengthen the specification and reduce future rework.

1) Missing non-functional requirements
- Performance targets: expected max latency for upload, processing, and search; throughput goals (e.g., documents per second); SLA for retry/backoff behavior.
- Scalability model: single-node vs. multi-node; shard/partition strategy for metadata and index; horizontal scaling plan.
- Availability and durability: replication factor for stored files and metadata; backup/restore cadence; disaster recovery RPO/RTO.
- Observability: metrics to collect (processing status, queue depth, retry counts, index size, search latency); logging standards; traces for end-to-end flows.
- Security and access control: authentication/authorization model for upload, search, and admin operations; data encryption at rest/in transit; tenant isolation if multi-tenant.
- Compliance: data retention policies, audit logging, and GDPR/CCPA considerations for uploaded content.

2) Ambiguities about processing semantics
- “Divides the text into searchable sections” is vague: define section granularity (e.g., paragraph, fixed tokens, or semantic units) and whether sectioning varies by format.
- “Create a searchable index” should specify index type (inverted index, BM25, embeddings; support for keyword and semantic search).
- Handling of non-text PDFs (scanned images): OCR requirements, quality thresholds, and fallback handling.
- Language support: multilingual documents; how language detection and stemming/lemmatization are handled.
- Consistency guarantees: eventual vs. strong consistency between stored file metadata and index; how updates or reprocessing affect search results.

3) Data model gaps
- Metadata fields: file hash, size, original name, MIME type, upload timestamp, user/owner, processing status, retry count, format version, preprocessing flags.
- Index metadata: per-section identifiers, offset ranges, confidence scores for OCR/NER, and provenance back to source document.
- Versioning and duplicates: exact deduplication strategy (hash-based, content-based, or delta-dup); whether file resequencing produces new records or updates existing ones.

4) Duplicate prevention and idempotency
- Repeated uploads: need a clear deduplication mechanism (content hash, precomputed fingerprint) and idempotent API semantics to avoid partial processing on retries.
- Race conditions: what happens if two uploads of the same file arrive concurrently? Ensure a canonical lock or idempotent path.

5) Extensibility for new formats
- The design should decouple format-specific extractors from the core pipeline; define an abstract extractor interface and a registry for format handlers.
- Provide a plug-in mechanism for new formats with minimal code changes and testing requirements.

6) Processing workflow clarity
- Async processing: specify the queuing model (enqueue on upload, worker pool, backpressure handling).
- Failure handling: retry policy (max retries, exponential backoff, jitter), dead-letter queue design, and alerting triggers.
- Progress visibility: how status is reported to users in real time or via polling; support for streaming status updates.

7) API surface gaps
- Upload API: constraints on metadata, chunked uploads for large files, resumable uploads, and checksum verification.
- Search API: query formats, ranking options, pagination, and result enrichment (sections with document IDs and offsets).
- Admin/debug endpoints: status, metrics, and reprocessing triggers.
- Observability endpoints: health checks and readiness probes.

8) Testing plan gaps
- Include testing at multiple levels: unit, integration with mock storage/index, end-to-end with sample documents (PDF/TXT/Markdown) including failure scenarios.
- Define test data sets (sizes, languages, OCR-heavy PDFs) and acceptance criteria for search results and processing statuses.
- Performance/stress tests and flaky-network simulations.

9) Deployment and maintainability
- Deployability: whether to use container orchestration, resource isolation between ingestion, processing, and indexing services.
- Configuration management: environment-specific settings, feature flags for format support, and secrets management.
- Team maintainability: clear coding standards, module boundaries, and documentation scaffolds.

10) Risk areas to address explicitly
- OCR accuracy vs. index quality; how low-quality OCR is handled or excluded.
- Consistency between file content and extracted text when updated metadata or fixes occur.
- Data localization constraints, if any, in multi-region deployments.

Suggestions to strengthen the prompt:
- Add explicit non-functional requirements (latency, throughput, durability, security).
- Specify deduplication strategy and idempotency guarantees.
- Require a pluggable format extractor interface with example extensions for PDF, TXT, Markdown, and an OCR fallback.
- Define the index type and search behavior (support for both keyword and semantic search).
- Include an explicit end-to-end example flow with timestamps and statuses to verify the workflow description.

- “Divides the text into searchable sections” lacks granularity. Define section granularity (e.g., paragraphs, sentences, fixed-length tokens) and whether it varies by format.

- Language support and normalization: clarify multilingual support, stemming/lemmatization, and language tagging for sections.
- Consistency guarantees: clarify whether index updates are eventual or strong relative to file metadata; how reprocessing updates reflect in search results.

3) Data model gaps
- Metadata: required fields such as file hash, size, original name, MIME type, upload timestamp, owner, processing status, retry counters, format version, preprocessing flags.
- Index metadata: identifiers for sections, offset ranges, confidence scores, provenance back to source, and versioning.
- Deduplication strategy: explicit approach (content hash, fingerprint, or delta), idempotency guarantees, and how to handle resequenced uploads.
- Versioning: how multiple reuploads or edits are represented (new version vs. update.

4) Duplicate prevention and idempotency
- Clarify idempotent API behavior for uploads; define canonical path for duplicates.
- Address race conditions: if two identical uploads arrive concurrently, ensure a single canonical record with safe parallel processing behavior.

5) Extensibility for new formats
- Require a pluggable extractor interface with a registry of format handlers; decouple extractors from core logic.
- Define a well-documented contract for adding new formats (PDF, TXT, Markdown, and future formats) with minimal changes.

6) Processing workflow clarity
- Asynchrony details: specify the queuing mechanism (message queue vs. task queue), worker pool sizing, and backpressure handling.
- Retry policy: max retries, exponential backoff, jitter, and dead-letter queue design; define alerting thresholds for repeated failures.
- Progress visibility: define how users query or subscribe to processing status; whether real-time streaming is supported or only polling.
- Ordering guarantees: is processing order important across steps, or can step results be out of order?

7) API surface gaps
- Upload API: chunked/resumable uploads, checksum verification, and metadata validation; support for large files.
- Search API: query syntax (boolean, phrase, fuzzy), ranking options, pagination, and result enrichment (document IDs, section anchors, confidence).
- Admin/debug endpoints: status, metrics, manual reindexing, and reprocessing triggers.
- Observability endpoints: health/readiness probes.

- Include unit, integration, and end-to-end tests with representative data sets (PDFs with/without OCR, TXT, Markdown, multilingual content).
- Define acceptance criteria for correctness of extraction, sectioning, indexing, and search results.
- Performance and reliability tests: load, spike, and failure simulations (network, storage outages, partial processing failures).

9) Deployment and maintainability
- Deployment model: containerized services, orchestration (Kubernetes), and resource isolation between ingestion, processing, and indexing.
- Configuration and secrets mgmt: environment-specific settings, feature flags, and secret rotation.
- Team ergonomics: clear module boundaries, coding standards, and documentation scaffolding to reduce onboarding friction.

10) Risk areas to address explicitly
- OCR accuracy vs. index quality; handling low-quality OCR or language detection errors.
- Consistency between stored content and extracted text during updates or fixes.
- Data localization and multi-region deployment constraints.

7) API surface gaps

8) Testing plan gaps

9) Deployment and maintainability

10) Risk areas to address explicitly

final_output:
1. System overview
This design describes a backend for a document-processing and search service supporting PDF, TXT, and Markdown uploads. It validates, stores, extracts text, segments into searchable units, builds an index, and enables search. Processing can be asynchronous with retryable steps. Deduplication prevents duplicates on repeated uploads. The system is designed to be extensible for new formats and maintainable for a small team.

2. Main components and responsibilities

| Component | Main Responsibility | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| API Gateway / Ingress | Authenticate requests, validate basic constraints, route to upload service | Upload request (file + metadata) | 202 accepted or error, upload job id |
| Upload Service | Validate file type/size, compute content hash, deduplicate, persist original file + metadata, enqueue processing | File, metadata | Storage reference, processing job enqueued |
| Storage Layer | Persist original file (blob store) and metadata; durable replicas | File bytes, metadata | File URL/id, metadata record |
| Extractor / Format Handlers | Extract text from supported formats; provide pluggable interface for new formats | Raw file, format metadata | Extracted text, per-format preprocessing flags |
| Sectionizer | Divide extracted text into searchable sections with stable identifiers | Extracted text | List of Section objects (section_id, document_id, text, offsets) |
| Indexer | Build and maintain search index (inverted index with optional embeddings) | Sections, metadata | Index structures (term → doc/section mappings) |
| Processing Orchestrator / Queue | Manage asynchronous processing steps, retries, and backoffs; track status | Processing job, status updates | Status events, progress state |
| Search Service | Accept queries, run against index, return ranked results with provenance | Query, optional filters | Results: document_id, section_id, text snippet, score |
| Status & Monitoring Service | Track processing status, retries, queue depth; expose health metrics | Internal signals | Health dashboards, alerts |
| Admin / Config Service | Manage configuration, feature flags, and format plugin registry | Admin requests | Status/config data |
| Consistency & Deduplication Store | Maintain mapping of file_hash → document record to enforce idempotency | Hash, metadata | Canonical document record, deduped reference |

3. End-to-end processing workflow

- Upload
  - Client uploads a file (PDF/TXT/Markdown) with optional owner/user identifier.
  - Upload Service validates mime-type and size, computes a content hash (e.g., SHA-256), and checks the Deduplication Store for an existing canonical record.
  - If duplicate exists, return existing document reference (idempotent path).
  - If new, persist the original file and metadata, create a processing job, and enqueue to the Processing Orchestrator.

- Processing pipeline (asynchronous)
  1) Validation step: verify file integrity (optional checksum on upload), confirm format support via registry.
  2) Text extraction: invoke the appropriate Format Handler (PDF/TXT/Markdown; OCR fallback for image-rich PDFs if configured).
  3) Sectioning: run Sectionizer to segment text into sections with stable identifiers.
  4) Indexing: update the inverted index (support both keyword-based; optionally embeddings for semantic search). Store section-level provenance.
  5) Status update: Processing Orchestrator records status transitions (PENDING → PROCESSING → DONE or FAILED) with retry counters and backoff.
  6) Notification: optional webhook or polling-based status retrieval for clients.

- Search
  - Clients submit search queries to the Search Service.
  - The service uses the index to locate relevant sections; applies language tagging, stemming where supported, and ranking (e.g., BM25 with optional embeddings ranking).
  - Returns top results with document_id, section_id, snippet, and score; supports pagination.

- Status reporting
  - Clients can query per-document status (Uploaded, Extracting, Sectioning, Indexing, DONE, FAILED) and retry counts.
  - Failures surface error codes and retry guidance.

4. REST API design

### API Table

| Method | Endpoint | Purpose | Main Request Data | Main Response Data |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| POST | /upload | Submit a file for processing | Multipart/form-data: file, owner_id, max_size, optional metadata | { document_id, status: "ACCEPTED" } |
| GET | /documents/{document_id} | Retrieve document metadata and current status | - | { document_id, status, hash, size, mime_type, uploaded_at, processing_status, retry_count, result_ref } |
| GET | /documents/{document_id}/progress | Get progress/status details | - | { document_id, stage, progress_percent, node, last_updated } |
| POST | /search | Execute a search query | { query, language, top_k, page } | { total, results: [{ document_id, section_id, snippet, score }] } |
| POST | /internal/reindex | Trigger reindexing for a document (admin) | { document_id } | { status } |
| GET | /health | Liveness | - | { status: "UP" } |
| GET | /metrics | Metrics endpoint | - | Metrics data (prometheus-like) |

Notes:
- Upload supports chunked/resumable uploads could be added later; initial design accepts complete file in one request or multipart stream.
- Search supports boolean/phrase queries; semantic ranking optional behind feature flag.
- All endpoints require authentication (tokens/OAuth) and respect tenant ownership if multi-tenant.

5. Data model

- Document
  - document_id (UUID)
  - owner_id
  - file_hash (SHA-256)
  - size
  - mime_type
  - original_name
  - uploaded_at (timestamp)
  - processing_status (PENDING, EXTRACTING, SECTIONING, INDEXING, DONE, FAILED)
  - retry_count
  - format_version
  - preprocessing_flags (e.g., OCR_enabled, language_detected)
  - result_ref (index_version_id or index_root)

- Section
  - section_id (string)
  - document_id
  - offset_start, offset_end (character offsets)
  - text
  - language
  - confidence (OCR/NER confidence if available)

- IndexEntry (inverted)
  - term
  - document_id
  - section_id
  - offset_start
  - score
  - provenance (source_document_version)

- Format Handler Registry
  - format_id (e.g., "application/pdf", "text/markdown", "text/plain")
  - extractor_instance
  - supported_features (OCR_required, language_support)

- Deduplication Store
  - file_hash → document_id (canonical mapping)
  - versioning_tag (if reuploads update existing or create new version)

6. Suggested project structure

- cmd/
  - app/ (main service startup; config)
- internal/
  - api/ (request/response models; handlers)
  - services/
    - upload/
    - extractor/
    - sectioner/
    - index/
    - processing/
    - search/
    - status/
  - registry/
    - format_registry.go (pluggable format handlers)
  - storage/
    - blob_store.go
    - metadata_store.go
  - queue/
    - worker.go (orchestration)
- pkg/
  - models/ (data structures)
  - errors/ (custom error types)
  - logger/ (structured logging)
- test/
  - unit/
  - integration/
  - e2e/
- config/
  - defaults.yaml
  - envs/

7. Error handling, retry, and duplicate prevention

- Deduplication
  - Compute file_hash at upload; check Deduplication Store for existing document_id.
  - If exists, return canonical document reference without reprocessing (idempotent path).
  - If a different upload with same hash arrives concurrently, use a lightweight locking mechanism or atomic upsert to ensure a single document_id is created.

- Processing retries
  - Each processing step (extract, section, index) has its own retry policy.
  - Use exponential backoff with jitter; cap at a maximum delay (e.g., 5 minutes).
  - On persistent failure, move to Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) and mark document_status = FAILED; trigger alerting.

- Failure handling
  - Partial failures should be idempotent; if reattempted, system detects already-processed sections and skips idempotently.
  - For non-recoverable errors (format not supported, corrupt file), set processing_status = FAILED with a descriptive error_code.

- Consistency and idempotency
  - Upserts should be atomic; ensure index updates reflect the canonical document version.
  - If a document is re-uploaded with same hash while processing is ongoing, coalesce requests to a single processing path.

8. Testing plan

- Unit tests
  - Extractor normalization for PDF/TXT/Markdown; section boundaries correctness.
  - Deduplication logic for identical hashes.
  - Sectionization correctness across formats.

- Integration tests
  - End-to-end with sample PDFs (with and without OCR), TXT, Markdown.
  - Verify that: upload → processing → indexing → search returns expected results.
  - Test retry paths: simulate transient failures in extraction or indexing.

- End-to-end tests
  - Large documents; multilingual samples; ensure search relevance and latency targets.

- Performance tests
  - Load test: simulate multiple concurrent uploads and searches.
  - Backpressure and queue depth under peak load.

- Failure/recovery tests
  - Simulated network/storage outages; verify DLQ and recovery after restart.
  - Concurrent duplicate uploads to verify deduplication holds.

9. Design tradeoffs

- Simplicity vs. scalability: Start with a single-service orchestration model and a pluggable format registry; if demand grows, split ingestion, processing, and indexing into microservices with dedicated queues.
- Indexing depth: Begin with keyword-based inverted index; optionally add embeddings-based semantic ranking behind a feature flag to manage complexity.
- Consistency: Favor eventual consistency between index and file metadata for speed; provide explicit versioning to resolve mismatches if needed.
- Extensibility: Emphasize a clean format extractor interface and a registry to minimize future code changes when adding new formats.

10. Design refinements to address risk

- Explicit non-functional targets (latency, throughput, durability, security) should be defined in project requirements and monitoring dashboards.
- Provide an idempotent, canonical path for duplicates and a safe locking mechanism for concurrent uploads.
- Require a pluggable extractor with concrete examples for PDFs (including OCR), TXT, Markdown, and an OCR fallback path.
- Support both keyword and semantic search with language-aware processing for multilingual documents.
- Document end-to-end example flows with timestamps and status checkpoints to verify workflow clarity.

Component Table

| Component | Main Responsibility | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| API Gateway | Authenticate and route | Upload request | Auth-token validated; routing decision |
| Upload Service | Validate, dedup, persist, enqueue | File + metadata | Document id; processing job enqueued |
| Storage Layer | Persist original file and metadata | File bytes, metadata | Storage references; metadata records |
| Extractor / Format Handlers | Extract text per format; OCR fallback | Raw file | Extracted text; flags |
| Sectionizer | Create sections from text | Extracted text | Sections (id, offsets, text) |
| Indexer | Build and maintain index | Sections + provenance | Inverted index structures |
| Processing Orchestrator | Manage async pipeline and retries | Processing job | Status updates; next step triggers |
| Search Service | Execute queries against index | Query | Ranked results with provenance |
| Status & Monitoring | Observability and health | System signals | Metrics, dashboards |
| Admin/Config | Manage format registry and config | Admin requests | Config/registry data |

API Table

| Method | Endpoint | Purpose | Main Request Data | Main Response Data |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| POST | /upload | Submit a file for processing | Multipart: file, owner_id, max_size | { document_id, status: "ACCEPTED" } |
| GET | /documents/{document_id} | Retrieve metadata/status | - | { document_id, status, hash, size, mime_type, uploaded_at } |
| GET | /documents/{document_id}/progress | Get progress details | - | { stage, progress_percent, last_updated } |
| POST | /search | Run a query | { query, language, top_k } | { total, results: [{ document_id, section_id, snippet, score }] } |
| GET | /health | Health check | - | { status: "UP" } |
